Final Project for BIOS 26211

Authors: Danny Liu and Daniel Gong

Background

Breast cancer treatment decisions depend on molecular biomarkers — ER status, HER2 amplification, histologic grade, and intrinsic subtype — traditionally assessed through immunohistochemistry or specialized assays. RNA-seq provides genome-wide expression profiles, raising the question: can machine learning on gene expression reliably predict these biomarkers?

Beyond prediction, we want to understand which genes carry the signal. Classical differential gene expression (DEG) analysis tests each gene individually, but this ignores relationships between genes. Regularized linear models like LASSO fit all genes jointly, identifying which genes matter given the others. Nonlinear models like gradient boosting can capture gene-gene interactions. By comparing these three approaches, we can determine whether the predictive signal lives in individual genes, joint linear combinations, or complex interactions.

Main questions:

  1. Which individual genes are differentially expressed between ER+ and ER- tumors?
  2. Which genes jointly predict biomarker status when modeled together?
  3. Do nonlinear gene-gene interactions improve prediction beyond linear models?

Danny: project design, code implementation, model training | Daniel: background research, biological interpretation, conclusions

References

  1. Brueffer et al. (2018). Clinical Value of RNA Sequencing-Based Classifiers for Prediction of the Five Conventional Breast Cancer Biomarkers. JNCI.
  2. Parker et al. (2009). Supervised Risk Predictor of Breast Cancer Based on Intrinsic Subtypes. JCO.
  3. Hastie, Tibshirani, Friedman. The Elements of Statistical Learning. Springer.
  4. James et al. An Introduction to Statistical Learning. Springer.

The Data

We use the GSE96058 dataset from the SCAN-B (Sweden Cancerome Analysis Network – Breast) initiative, which profiled 3,273 primary breast tumors using RNA-seq. The expression matrix contains ~30,000 genes per sample. Clinical annotations include ER status, HER2 status, NHG histologic grade (1-3), and PAM50 intrinsic subtype (Luminal A, Luminal B, Her2-enriched, Basal, Normal-like).

Target variable distributions:

  • ER status: 2,935 positive vs 254 negative (220 missing)
  • HER2 status: 2,843 negative vs 438 positive (128 missing)
  • NHG grade: G1: 505, G2: 1,593, G3: 1,246 (65 missing)
  • PAM50 subtype: LumA: 1,709, LumB: 767, Basal: 360, Her2: 348, Normal: 225

Figure 1: Distribution of the four target variables across the cohort.

Data cleaning pipeline:

  • Sentinel values (log2(0.1) = -3.32) treated as missing — none found after filtering
  • Genes with >50% NaN removed, then variance-based filter retaining top 5,000 genes
  • 80/20 stratified train/test split for each target, preserving class proportions

Danny: data loading, cleaning pipeline, train/test split design

Methods

We use three progressively sophisticated approaches to identify predictive genes:

  1. Regularized Logistic Regression (LASSO & Ridge): fit all genes jointly with regularization
  2. Differential Gene Expression (DEG): test each gene individually (classical approach)
  3. HistGradientBoosting: capture nonlinear gene-gene interactions

We first perform exploratory data analysis to understand the structure of the expression space.

Exploratory Data Analysis

Figure 2: PCA of gene expression colored by ER status and PAM50 subtype. ER-negative (Basal) tumors separate clearly along PC1.

Figure 3: Correlation heatmap of the 50 most variable genes.

Figure 4: Expression of canonical marker genes across PAM50 subtypes.

Key EDA observations:

  • PCA shows clear separation of ER-negative (Basal) tumors along PC1, consistent with known breast cancer biology.
  • PAM50 subtypes form partially overlapping clusters: Basal is most distinct; Luminal A and B overlap considerably.
  • ESR1 shows highest expression in Luminal subtypes and lowest in Basal, confirming ER status should be highly predictable.
  • ERBB2 expression is elevated specifically in Her2-enriched tumors.

Danny: EDA implementation

Method 1: Regularized Logistic Regression (LASSO & Ridge)

We train Ridge (L2) and LASSO (L1) logistic regression on all ~5,000 genes simultaneously using 10-fold stratified cross-validation. Unlike DEG, these methods account for correlations between genes — LASSO zeros out redundant features while Ridge shrinks all coefficients.

ER Status Results:

  • Best Ridge: C=0.001, CV AUC = 0.9748
  • Best LASSO: C=0.1, CV AUC = 0.9710
  • LASSO selected 162 / 5,000 non-zero coefficients

Figure 5: Ridge vs LASSO performance comparison for ER status prediction.

We then applied Ridge and LASSO to all four targets: ER status, HER2 status, NHG grade, and PAM50 subtype.

Figure 6: Cross-validation AUC across all targets and model types.

Danny: model implementation, tuning, and multi-target pipeline

Method 2: Differential Gene Expression Analysis

We perform genome-wide differential expression testing between ER+ and ER- tumors using two-sample t-tests for each of the ~5,000 genes. We apply Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction and visualize results with a volcano plot. This is the classical single-gene approach — we compare its gene selections with LASSO's joint selections.

  • Significant genes (adj_p < 0.05): 3,531 / 5,000
  • Significant AND |log2FC| > 1: 1,273 genes

Figure 7: Volcano plot showing log2 fold change vs. -log10(adjusted p-value) for all genes.

Danny: implementation | Daniel: interpretation

Method 3: HistGradientBoosting (Nonlinear)

Linear models assume class boundaries are hyperplanes. Gradient boosting builds decision trees that naturally capture gene-gene interactions — if gene A is high AND gene B is low, predict a different class. If HistGBoost outperforms LASSO, it means gene combinations carry signal beyond individual genes.

  • HistGBoost (full features): CV AUC = 0.9750
  • HistGBoost (DEG features, 1,123 genes): CV AUC = 0.9742
  • Ridge LogReg: CV AUC = 0.9748
  • LASSO LogReg: CV AUC = 0.9710

HistGradientBoosting matched but did not outperform linear models, confirming that the biomarker boundaries in expression space are approximately linear.

Danny: implementation and comparison

Results

Results 1: Test Set Evaluation & Statistical Validation

We evaluate the best model per target on held-out test data:

TargetTest AUCTest Accuracy
ER0.956196.75%
HER20.911792.71%
NHG0.839968.43%
PAM500.984788.70%

Figure 8: ROC curves for the best model per target on held-out test data.

Figure 9: Confusion matrices for all four targets on the test set.

Figure 10: Permutation test for ER status — observed AUC vs. null distribution (100 permutations, p < 0.01).

Danny: test evaluation, statistical testing

Results 2: Feature Selection & Biological Interpretation

Figure 11: LASSO coefficient path showing how gene coefficients change with regularization strength.

Figure 12: Expression heatmap of top 30 LASSO-selected genes, ordered by PAM50 subtype.

Figure 13: Feature importance from HistGradientBoosting (ER status).

Figure 14: Full comparison of Ridge vs LASSO vs HistGradientBoosting for ER status.

Biological interpretation of LASSO-selected genes:

  • ER status: ESR1 and its co-regulators (GATA3, FOXA1, TFF1/3) are top predictive genes — well-established markers of the luminal phenotype. Negative coefficients for FOXC1 and basal keratins (KRT5, KRT17) mark ER-negative basal-like tumors.
  • HER2 status: ERBB2 (the HER2 gene itself) and GRB7 (same amplicon on chr17q12) appear as top features.
  • NHG grade: Proliferation genes (MKI67, TOP2A, CCNB1) dominate, consistent with grading criteria that assess mitotic rate.

Danny: feature selection analysis | Daniel: biological interpretation

Conclusions

  1. Individual gene testing (DEG) identified hundreds of differentially expressed genes between ER+ and ER- tumors, dominated by known markers (ESR1, GATA3, ERBB2).
  2. Joint linear modeling (LASSO/Ridge) achieved CV AUC > 0.97 for ER status, with LASSO selecting a sparse set of genes that overlap strongly with the DEG results. This confirms that the predictive signal is captured by a small number of biologically meaningful genes.
  3. Nonlinear modeling (HistGradientBoosting) matched but did not outperform LASSO, indicating that gene-gene interactions do not substantially improve prediction. The biomarker boundaries in expression space are approximately linear.
  4. Cross-method convergence: all three approaches identify the same core gene sets (ESR1/GATA3/FOXA1 for ER, ERBB2/GRB7 for HER2, MKI67/TOP2A for grade), providing strong evidence of genuine biological signal.
  5. Generalization: the approach works across biomarkers (ER, HER2, NHG, PAM50), with performance scaling with target complexity.

New Questions Raised:

  • Would these results replicate on independent cohorts (TCGA, METABRIC)?
  • Can pathway-level features (gene set enrichment) provide more interpretable models?
  • Would integrating clinical features (age, tumor size) improve predictions for harder targets like NHG?

Limitations:

  • Single dataset (SCAN-B) — external validation needed
  • DEG analysis limited to ER status (binary); multiclass targets would need ANOVA
  • Gene expression only — no clinical covariates integrated
  • Variance-based gene filter may miss low-variance predictive genes

Daniel: conclusions and interpretation

  1. Brueffer et al. (2018). Clinical Value of RNA Sequencing-Based Classifiers for Prediction of the Five Conventional Breast Cancer Biomarkers. JNCI. doi:10.1093/jnci/djy156
